util Interface Derivable All Superinterfaces: Nameable All Known Subinterfaces: Instantiable All Known Implementing Classes: InstantiableNamedObj , NamedObj public interface Derivable extends Nameable This interface is for objects that can be derived

This interface is for objects that can be derived. A derived object is "inherited" via the class mechanism in Ptolemy II and tracks the object from which it is derived. That object is its "prototype." The derived object is usually created in its

pe." The derived object is usually created in its container as a side effect of the creation of the prototype. The method propagateExistence() is the mechanism by which derived objects are created. The prototype and its derived objects are instan

. The prototype and its derived objects are instances of the same Java class, and by default, if they have a "value," then they have the same value. The derived object may, however, "override" its value. Propagation of changes in the value from th

alue. Propagation of changes in the value from the prototype to the derived objects that do not override the value is handled by the propagateValue() method. It is up to that method to properly handle shadowing that might occur if a derived obje

andle shadowing that might occur if a derived object that is overridden is also a prototype for other derived objects. A derived object arises from a parent-child relationship, which is supported by the subinterface Instantiable. Every object that

the subinterface Instantiable. Every object that is (deeply) contained by a parent has a corresponding derived object that is (deeply) contained by the child. Thus, the existence of the derived object is "implied" by the parent-child relationship

ject is "implied" by the parent-child relationship. The depth of a derived object relative to this parent-child relationship is returned by the getDerivedLevel() method. For example, if the container of a derived object is the child of the contai

er of a derived object is the child of the container of its prototype, then getDerivedLevel() will return 1. The derived level returned by getDerivedLevel() affects whether a derived object is explicitly mentioned when a model is exported to MoML

icitly mentioned when a model is exported to MoML. Normally, a derived object is not mentioned in the MoML since its existence is "implied" by the existence of the prototype and the parent-child relationship. However, since it is possible to expo

lationship. However, since it is possible to export MoML for any object in a hierarchical model, the exported MoML may not include the parent-child relationship, since it might be below it in the hierarchy. In this case, the derived object will

ierarchy. In this case, the derived object will be described in the exported MoML, since it is not implied in the exported MoML. The getDerivedList() method returns a list of all the objects that are derived from a prototype. The getPrototypeList

are derived from a prototype. The getPrototypeList() method returns a list of all prototypes from which this object is derived. Since: Ptolemy II 4.0 Version: $Id: Derivable.java,v 1.23 2005/07/08 19:59:16 cxh Exp $ Author: Edward A. Lee See Also: I

DerivedList public java.util.List getDerivedList () Return a list of objects derived from this one. This is the list of objects that are "inherited" by their containers from a container of this object. The existence of these derived objects is

ject. The existence of these derived objects is "implied" by a parent-child relationship somewhere above this object in the containment hierarchy. Implementors of this method may return an empty list, but should not return null. This method sho

is called. getPrototypeList public java.util.List getPrototypeList () throws IllegalActionException Return a list of prototypes for this object. The list is ordered so that more local prototypes are listed before mo

so that more local prototypes are listed before more remote prototypes. A prototype is more local if the parent-child relationship is deeper in the containment hierarchy. Returns: A list of prototypes for this object, each of which is assured

ypes for this object, each of which is assured of being an instance of the same (Java) class as this object, or an empty list if there are no prototypes. Throws: IllegalActionException - If a prototype with the right name but the wrong class
